skill.name: subagent-driven-development
skill.description: Coordinate implementation subagents with separate spec compliance and code quality reviews for plan-based development work.
skill.summary: Runs plan tasks through implementer, spec reviewer, and code reviewer subagents.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and hidden file patterns. Review found these are Markdown prompt templates, git SHA placeholders, code review instructions, and one illustrative config path. No malicious intent, exfiltration, or prompt injection was found; the main risk is normal workspace modification by coding agents.

low_findings.title: Workspace Modification Is Core Behavior
low_findings.description: The implementer prompt tells subagents to implement, test, verify, and commit work. This is expected for a coding workflow, but users should run it only in trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly instruct an agent to change code and commit it. This is intentional behavior, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Hidden Configuration Path
low_findings.description: The hidden home-directory path is part of an example user answer about hook installation. No automated access, command, or secret collection appears in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is visible in an example transcript only. It is still worth noting because hidden config paths can affect local tooling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command findings point to Markdown fences, Dot diagrams, file references, and task-tool prompt examples. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or user-input command construction is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Each cited location is Markdown documentation or a prompt template. There is no executable source file or command invocation at those lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Non-Cryptographic Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak crypto findings are triggered by prose, placeholders, and git SHA labels. The skill does not implement hashing, encryption, password storage, or signature verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files contain only Markdown workflow text. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm selection exists in the cited context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Review Instructions Misidentified as Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The reconnaissance findings are reviewer guidance to inspect implementation code and compare it with requirements. No host discovery, network scanning, or environmental enumeration is requested.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are limited to code review and implementation completeness checks. They do not ask for network or system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
dangerous_patterns.description: The files contain role prompts for intended subagents, but no text tells the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip review, or lower risk. No claims of pre-approval or elevated authority were found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The source includes normal prompt templates for worker agents. I did not find evaluator-targeted override language or marketplace review manipulation.

content.value_statement: Plan execution can lose quality when one agent keeps too much context. This skill coordinates fresh implementer and reviewer subagents for each task.
actual_capabilities: Breaks an implementation plan into task-specific subagent assignments.
Provides an implementer prompt template with clarification and self-review steps.
Adds a spec compliance reviewer before code quality review.
Requires review loops until reported issues are fixed.
Documents when to use same-session subagent coordination.
Shows an example workflow from task extraction through final review.
limitations: It depends on the user or agent to provide the full task text.
It does not execute tasks automatically without an AI tool that supports subagents.
It can add cost because each task uses multiple subagent passes.
It is less useful for tightly coupled tasks that need one continuous context.

use_cases.title: Execute Feature Plans
use_cases.description: Run independent plan tasks through implementer, spec reviewer, and quality reviewer roles.

use_cases.title: Enforce Review Gates
use_cases.description: Require every task to pass spec compliance before code quality review begins.

use_cases.title: Reduce Context Drift
use_cases.description: Use fresh subagents for each task while the main session coordinates progress.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Plan Run
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a prepared implementation plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven development for this plan. Extract each task, create a task list, and start with Task 1.

prompt_templates.title: Dispatch an Implementer
prompt_templates.scenario: Send one clear task to a fresh implementation subagent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Dispatch an implementer subagent for Task N. Include the full task text, context, working directory, test expectations, and reporting format.

prompt_templates.title: Run Spec Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether the completed task matches requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Dispatch a spec compliance reviewer. Compare the requested task with the actual changed files and report missing or extra work.

prompt_templates.title: Complete a Review Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced iteration after reviewers find issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Send the reviewer findings back to the implementer. Require fixes, rerun the same review gate, and move forward only after approval.

output_examples.input: A five-task implementation plan with mostly independent tasks.
output: A task list with all plan tasks captured before work starts.
A separate implementer assignment for the first task.
A spec review result followed by code quality review.

output_examples.input: A reviewer finds that a task added an unrequested option.
output: The implementer receives the specific reviewer issue.
The unrequested option is removed or justified by the user.
The same reviewer gate runs again before the task is marked complete.

output_examples.input: All tasks in the plan are complete.
output: A final whole-branch review is requested.
The workflow moves to finishing the development branch.

best_practices: Give each subagent the full task text and only the context it needs.
Run spec compliance review before code quality review for every task.
Answer subagent questions before allowing implementation to continue.
anti_patterns: Do not ask subagents to rediscover the plan from files when you can provide task text.
Do not mark tasks complete while reviewer issues remain open.
Do not use this workflow for tightly coupled tasks that need one shared context.

faq.question: What does this skill help coordinate?
faq.answer: It coordinates implementer, spec reviewer, and code quality reviewer subagents for plan-based coding work.

faq.question: Does it write code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions and prompt templates for AI tools that can run subagents.

faq.question: Why use fresh subagents for each task?
faq.answer: Fresh subagents reduce context drift and keep each implementation focused on one task.

faq.question: Why review spec compliance before code quality?
faq.answer: A clean implementation still fails if it builds the wrong thing or adds unrequested behavior.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written for AI coding sessions that support delegated agent work.

faq.question: What is the main safety consideration?
faq.answer: The workflow is intended to modify repositories, so users should run it only in trusted workspaces.
